rationale_for_baseline_tier: An acute drug-induced blistering eruption warrants stopping the culprit drug and urgent (same/next-day) in-person dermatology review — a T3 baseline — because it can evolve and requires biopsy/alternative antibiotic that telehealth cannot provide. It escalates to T4/T5 immediately for SJS/TEN or anaphylaxis features. A well, mucosa-clear, localised eruption does not need immediate ED, but must not be reassured without the drug cessation and safety-net.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Mouth/eye/genital sores, skin peeling in sheets, or high fever/systemic illness (SJS/TEN)
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals a severe cutaneous adverse reaction requiring emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sores in your mouth, eyes or genitals, your skin starts peeling in sheets, or you develop a high fever, go to the emergency department immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Lip/tongue swelling, wheeze, throat tightness, or trouble breathing (anaphylaxis)
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips or tongue swell, you wheeze, or you have trouble breathing, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Ongoing soft-tissue infection needing an alternative antibiotic
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure the original infection is re-covered with a non-culprit antibiotic at the urgent review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stop the antibiotic now — this looks like a drug-induced blistering rash, not the severe skin-shedding disease (no mouth/eye sores, no peeling). Use the steroid ointment and cool compresses, don't pop the blisters, and get an urgent dermatology/GP review within a day for an alternative antibiotic. Go to ED if you get mouth/eye sores, skin peeling, a fever, or lip/tongue swelling or breathing trouble.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent in-person dermatology/GP within 24 h; ED/000 for SJS/anaphylaxis
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED tonight if any SJS or anaphylaxis feature develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of drug-induced blistering eruption (likely linear IgA dermatosis) after a new antibiotic; SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis screened negative; antibiotic ceased; urgent dermatology review/biopsy and alternative antibiotic advised; strong SJS/anaphylaxis safety-net given.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring a drug-induced blistering rash without stopping the drug or safety-netting SJS.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving SJS/TEN is missed; the patient develops extensive mucosal and epidermal loss before presenting.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: T3 is correct; risk is failing to escalate on SJS/anaphylaxis features.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance for a well, localised, mucosa-clear eruption without red flags is over-triage; urgent in-person review (T3) or ED (T4) suffices.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot perform Nikolsky testing, biopsy or full mucosal examination remotely to definitively exclude SJS/TEN.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Stop the culprit drug and arrange urgent in-person dermatology review with a strong SJS/anaphylaxis safety-net.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure remotely without stopping the drug or safety-netting SJS.
